full_response:
Step–by–step discussion of one empirical failure of Newtonian gravity  

1. Newton’s law of universal gravitation predicts that the motion of a test particle in the static gravitational field of a point mass is exactly Keplerian.  
   • In particular, in the two–body problem the trajectory is a closed ellipse whose perihelion (point of closest approach) is fixed in space.  
   • Mathematically, in Newtonian mechanics the Runge–Lenz vector  
     A⃗ = p⃗ × L⃗  − m k r̂     (k = GMm)  
     is an exact constant of motion, guaranteeing a fixed orientation of the ellipse.

2. The orbit of Mercury about the Sun is therefore predicted, after taking into account the perturbing Newtonian forces of the other planets, to precess by  
      Δϖ_Newton ≃ 531″ per century.  
   This value comes entirely from the calculated Newtonian perturbations.

3. The observed advance of Mercury’s perihelion, measured astronomically, is  
      Δϖ_obs = 574″ per century.  

4. The discrepancy is  
      Δϖ_extra = Δϖ_obs − Δϖ_Newton ≃ 43″ per century.  

5. No adjustment of Newtonian parameters (e.g., unmodelled asteroid belts, solar oblateness, a new inner planet “Vulcan,” etc.) could account for this residual 43″/century.  

6. General Relativity, however, predicts an additional precession  
      Δϖ_GR = 6πGM⊙/(a(1−e²)c²) = 43″ per century,  
   where a and e are Mercury’s semi-major axis and eccentricity. This exactly matches the observed excess.

Conclusion  
The failure of Newtonian gravity to explain the remaining 43 arc-seconds per century in Mercury’s perihelion advance is a clear empirical discrepancy remedied by General Relativity.

\boxed{\text{Newtonian gravity cannot account for the extra } \approx 43^{\prime\prime}\text{/century advance of Mercury’s perihelion that is precisely explained by General Relativity.}}  

Confidence: 88%
